stem: How many edges does the complete bipartite graph \( K_{[[m]],[[n]]} \) have?
solution: Every one of the \( [[m]] \) vertices on one side is joined to every one of the \( [[n]] \) vertices on the other side, giving \( [[m]] \cdot [[n]] = [[answer]] \) edges.
distractor_rationales: This adds the two part sizes instead of multiplying them.
This treats all \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) vertices as one complete graph, adding edges within each side that do not exist.
This sums the squares of the part sizes rather than their product.
This squares the total number of vertices.
This overcounts by the size of one part.
This undercounts by the size of one part.

stem: A complete bipartite graph has \( [[m]] \) vertices on one side and \( [[E]] \) edges in total. How many vertices are on the other side?
solution: Since the two sides have \( [[m]] \) and \( n \) vertices, the edge count satisfies \( [[m]] \cdot n = [[E]] \), so \( n = [[E]]/[[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the given side size instead of dividing by it.
This multiplies instead of dividing, growing far past a reasonable vertex count.
This adds the given side size instead of dividing by it.
This just repeats the given side size instead of solving for the other one.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: Start with the complete bipartite graph \( K_{[[m]],[[n]]} \), whose two parts have \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \) vertices. Now add every possible edge WITHIN each part (so no two vertices of the same part are left unjoined), turning the graph into the complete graph on all \( [[m]] + [[n]] \) vertices. How many new edges were added?
solution: The new edges are exactly the within-part pairs: \( \binom{[[m]]}{2} \) pairs on the first side plus \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} \) pairs on the second side, since the between-part edges already existed. So the count is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the edge count of the FULL complete graph \( K_{[[m]]+[[n]]} \), including the original bipartite edges, not just the new ones.
This is the number of edges that already existed in \( K_{[[m]],[[n]]} \), not the ones added.
This only counts the new edges within the first part, forgetting the second part.
This only counts the new edges within the second part, forgetting the first part.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
